title: Gouache as a calligraphy medium for illuminated letters
subtitle: Thinning, loading, and working with opaque watercolour on pen and brush
excerpt: Calligraphy gouache, opaque watercolour thinned to ink consistency, allows colour work and white lettering on dark grounds that ink alone cannot achieve. The medium is unforgiving of wrong consistency: too thick and it drags the nib; too thin and it loses covering power. This tutorial covers mixing and loading, with notes on correction.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on colour, Johnston gives detailed guidance on the use of gouache and distemper for illuminated work, describing the right consistency and the tests to apply. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

glossaryTerms.definition: Opaque watercolour made with a higher pigment load than transparent watercolour, plus a white filler (typically chalk) that increases opacity. Dries to a matte, non-reflective surface.
Beaten and clarified egg white, used historically as a medium for pigments and as a size for gold leaf. Modern calligraphers sometimes substitute gum arabic.

content.text: is the standard illumination medium in the European manuscript tradition. Where ink is transparent and thin, gouache is opaque and covers, white on black vellum, gold-yellow over red, blue over green. Johnston's illuminated capitals use it throughout. The challenge is getting the consistency right: the medium needs to flow through a dip pen at the viscosity of single cream, yet cover the paper completely in one pass.

items.name: Calligraphy gouache (designers' gouache), titanium white and one or two colours
items.substitutions: Artist-grade watercolour with added Chinese white; both are less opaque than designers' gouache

items.name: Distilled water (not tap water, which can affect pigment stability)

content.text: Squeeze a pea-sized amount of gouache onto the palette. Add distilled water drop by drop, mixing with a brush until the mixture flows easily, somewhere between single cream and semi-skimmed milk.

Test: dip the pen and make a stroke. The gouache should flow readily and cover fully. If it drags, thin slightly. If it runs, thicken by allowing some water to evaporate for a few minutes and re-testing.
For better adhesion on smooth hot-press paper, add two or three drops of
to the mix. It improves grip without affecting the consistency much.

Load the nib by dipping the underside (the back of the nib, not the writing face) into the gouache, or by loading with a fine brush from above. Dipping from the underside keeps the writing face clean.
Work at the same nib angle as for ink, gouache follows the same stroke rules as ink for broad-edge calligraphy. The stroke feels slightly softer and more resistant than ink, which is normal.
Reload the nib more frequently than you would with ink, gouache dries faster in the nib and may block the slit after three or four strokes.

Gouache is water-soluble when wet and can be re-wetted and lifted when dry. A clean damp brush applied to a dry error will re-dissolve it; then blot and let dry before re-writing. On vellum, a slightly damp knife blade can scrape dry gouache away without damaging the surface.

items.symptom: Gouache flakes off the paper after drying
items.cause: Too much pigment, too little water; the dried film is too thick.
items.fix: Thin with more water and test again. Properly thinned gouache dries flat and adheres.

items.symptom: Strokes look semi-transparent rather than opaque
items.cause: Too much water; the opacity of the medium is diluted.
items.fix: Thicken by allowing some water to evaporate from the palette, or add a little fresh gouache from the tube.

items.symptom: Nib clogs after two or three strokes
items.cause: The gouache is drying too fast in the nib in a warm, dry room.
items.fix: Reload every stroke or two rather than waiting. Clean the nib with a soft cloth between letters.
